questions.question: In a bipartite graph, what guarantees that a matching is maximum?
options: Every left vertex is matched
No augmenting path exists with respect to the matching
The matching has size |V|/2
All edges are saturated by the matching
questions.explanation: Berge's theorem states that a matching M is maximum if and only if there is no augmenting path — an alternating path that starts and ends at unmatched vertices. Flipping an augmenting path increases |M| by 1, so its absence certifies maximality.

questions.question: König's theorem establishes which equality for bipartite graphs?
options: Maximum flow = minimum cut
Maximum matching size = minimum vertex cover size
Maximum independent set = minimum edge cover size
Chromatic number = maximum degree + 1
questions.explanation: König's theorem: in any bipartite graph, the size of a maximum matching equals the size of a minimum vertex cover. This is proved by constructing a flow network and applying the max-flow min-cut theorem.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the Hopcroft-Karp algorithm for maximum bipartite matching on a graph with V vertices and E edges?
options: O(V · E)
O(E · √V)
O(V² · E)
O(E · log V)
questions.explanation: Hopcroft-Karp runs in O(E√V). It finds a maximal set of shortest augmenting paths per phase using BFS layered-graph + DFS. At most √V phases suffice, and each phase scans all edges once in BFS and once in DFS.

questions.question: In the Gale-Shapley deferred-acceptance algorithm, which property is proven to hold?
options: The result is stable and optimal for the receivers (women)
The result is stable and optimal for the proposers (men)
The result may be unstable but is always Pareto-optimal
The result depends on the order proposals are processed
questions.explanation: Gale-Shapley always produces a stable matching that is man-optimal (each man gets his best possible stable partner) and woman-pessimal (each woman gets her worst stable partner). The order of proposal processing within a round does not affect the final matching.

questions.question: In a bipartite graph, what is a maximum matching?
options: A set of edges that covers every vertex exactly once
A largest set of edges with no two sharing a vertex
A set of edges that connects every pair of vertices
A minimum weight spanning tree
questions.explanation: A matching is a set of edges where no two share an endpoint. A maximum matching is one with the most edges possible. In bipartite graphs, Hopcroft-Karp finds this in O(E*sqrt(V)), and König's theorem relates it to minimum vertex cover.
